content.title: York River (Virginia)
content.content: The York River is a navigable estuary, approximately 34 miles (55 km) long, in eastern Virginia in the United States. It ranges in width from 1 mile (1.6 km) at its head to 2.5 miles (4.0 km) near its mouth on the west side of the Chesapeake Bay. Its watershed drains an area of the coastal plain of Virginia north and east of Richmond.

content.title: Association of Polish Electrical Engineers
content.content: The Association of Polish Electrical Engineers is a Polish non-governmental organisation integrating the community of electricians of Polish origin worldwide. The organisation brings together both engineers and technicians, as well as young students in electrical engineering in the broadest sense.

content.title: Cem Karaca (footballer)
content.content: Cem Karaca is a Turkish-German football coach and former player who played as a midfielder.

content.title: Aurora
content.content: An aurora is a natural light display in Earth's upper atmosphere caused by charged particles from the Sun colliding with atoms in the atmosphere. These collisions excite oxygen and nitrogen, which then emit light of different colors such as green, red, and purple. When observed in high-latitude regions they are called polar lights and aurora polaris. In the Arctic they are called the northern lights or aurora borealis; in the Antarctic, the term southern lights or aurora australis is used. Auroras display dynamic patterns of radiant light that appear as curtains, rays, spirals or dynamic flickers covering the entire sky.

content.title: Auroras from Space
content.content: What do auroras look like from above? Behold!  From the ground, auroras dance high above clouds, frequently causing spectacular displays.  From space, they look a bit different. As the International Space Station (ISS) circles the Earth every 90 minutes, it sometimes sees auroras below that are active on the night side.  A one-hour time-lapse video showing auroras from above was captured about two weeks ago from the orbiting ISS.  The ISS -- and all objects in low Earth orbit -- will pass well above green auroras but just a bit above red glowing auroras.  The auroras' electron and proton streams are too thin to be a danger to the ISS, just as clouds pose little danger to airplanes.  From orbit, as magnetic fields change, auroras can appear to squiggle and crawl like giant snakes.  The featured one-minute video was captured by French astronaut Sophie Adenot of the ISS Expedition 74 crew.    Sky Surprise: What picture did APOD feature on your birthday? (after 1995)
